EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Pacific storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to all of southeast Idaho.  One to three feet of snow fell in the Central Mountains with 4 to 8 inches in the Snake River Plain with blizzard conditions.  One to two feet were common in the southern and southeast highlands with up to 32 inches in the Upper Snake Highlands.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed east of Declo.  State Highways 39,32,37,46,21, 81, and 77 were all closed.  US Highways 20 and 26 were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained 40 mph winds with gusts over 60 mph at the Pocatello Regional Airport caused blizzard conditions for several hours on Christmas morning.  Interstate 86 was closed from Pocatello west to the Interstate 84 intersection due to the strong winds and drifting snow.   Snow amounts were 7 inches in Chubbuck, Pocatello, and Blackfoot with  6.5 inches at the Pocatello Regional Airport.  3 to 4 foot drifts were common in the Pocatello area.  State Highway 37 from American Falls to the Oneida/Power County line was closed due to drifting snow.  State Highway 39 from American Falls to 12 miles east of Aberdeen was closed.  Power lines were downed due to the winds 6 miles northeast of American Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Caribou Highlands with spotters reporting 16 inches 7 miles east of Pocatello, and 18 inches in Bone.  Heavy SNOTEL amounts were 21 inches at Sedgwick Peak, and 9 inches at Sheep Mountain and Somsen Ranch.

The second slide occurred at mile marker 133.79 which hit a truck and trailer. The third slide at mile marker 137.06 was large enough to flow into the Lochsa River and dam up the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 8-9, a winter storm moved east across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall. The build up of snow on the mountains caused it to become unstable and produce avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two avalanches were reported along Highway 12. The first, at mile marker 133.79, covered the highway with 3 to 4 feet of debris and snow. The second, at mile marker 132.48, covered the highway with 23 feet of snow, large trees and debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved over the Camas Prairie and the Whitebird Grade, producing accumulating heavy snow. Four inches of heavy wet snow was received in 1 hour and 45 minutes in Grangeville, with a total snow accumulation of 4.6 inches reported by spotters in 3 hours. Very slippery conditions caused vehicles to spin out over the Whitebird Grade, blocking traffic on U.S. Highway 95 between 6 PM to 725 PM, when the highway was temporarily closed. Idaho County Sheriff reported other vehicle slide offs and accidents due to the heavy wet accumulating snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary arctic front boundary resulted in record setting snow accumulations across portions of Northern Idaho.  The Coeur D'Alene area was hardest hit, where a record two day total of 33 inches was recorded.  An impressive 25 inches fell in a 24 hour period, which was also a record.  Athol was also hard hit where 30 inches of snow fell.  The heavy snow closed several roads, schools, businesses, and led to numerous accidents.  One such accident closed down west bound lanes of Interstate 90 near Coeur D'Alene for two hours.  Other snow accumulations from this event were noteworthy as well.  In the North Idaho Panhandle including the Coeur D'Alene area; 32 inches fell in Rathdrum, 29 inches three miles east of Spirit Lake, 29 inches in Post falls, 28 inches in Rathdrum, 23 inches in Blanchard, 13 inches in Clark Fork, and 8 inches in Bonners Ferry.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains; 17 inches fell five miles west of St. Maries, with 14 inches two miles south of Deary.  In the Idaho Palouse; 21 inches fell five miles north of Moscow, with much less accumulation south of here.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willow wildland fire use fire began with a lightning strike on July 14th and was allowed to continue to burn through September 25th.  It burned 4,510 acres of timber. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prescribed burn of 250 acres jumped to 800 acres due to unexpected winds jumping containment lines.  The fire located near Galena closed State Highway 75 for 4 hours on September 12th from 6 pm to 10 pm between Galena Summit and Stanley.  The fire was contained on September 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Flag Knoll wildland fire use fire began with a lightning strike on July 21st and was allowed to burn through September 3rd when rain ended it.  It burned over 500 acres of timer and brush.  No damages were caused by the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flag Knoll wildland fire use fire began with a lightning strike on July 21st and was allowed to burn through September 3rd when rain ended it.  The fire was located about 13 miles south of Swan Valley.  It burned over 500 acres of timer and brush.  No damages were caused by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging cold front moved through Southeast Oregon into Southwest Idaho knocking down trees and power lines around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 53 MPH was recorded at the National Weather Service office in Boise and local media and trained spotters reported trees down and power out in Boise, Mountain Home, Garden City and Kuna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 10 and 11, a strong Pacific storm system moved southeast across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall amounts in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observers in Powell and Dixie reported 8 and 9 inches of new snowfall accumulations, respectively, from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 10 and 11, a strong Pacific storm system moved southeast across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall amounts in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observers in Pierce and Elk City reported 10 inches of new snowfall accumulations from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season storm system moved through Southeast Oregon and most of southern Idaho creating windy and snowy conditions over the area. Parts of the Western Magic Valley and Southern Twin Falls County received four to five inches of snow in the valleys and up to nine inches in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported four to five inches of snow in Jerome, Twin Falls and surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season storm system moved through Southeast Oregon and most of southern Idaho creating windy and snowy conditions over the area. Parts of the Western Magic Valley and Southern Twin Falls County received four to five inches of snow in the valleys and up to nine inches in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported four to fives inches of snow around the Rogerson area and six to nine inches of snow were measured in the mountains at the Magic Mountain and Wilson Creek Snotels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 8 and 9, a shortwave trough moved through the Clearwater mountains producing heavy snow across Clearwater county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters in Weippe and Headquarters reported between 7.5 and 8 inches of new snowfall accumulations respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary arctic front boundary resulted in record setting snow accumulations across portions of Northern Idaho.  The Coeur D'Alene area was hardest hit, where a record two day total of 33 inches was recorded.  An impressive 25 inches fell in a 24 hour period, which was also a record.  Athol was also hard hit where 30 inches of snow fell.  The heavy snow closed several roads, schools, businesses, and led to numerous accidents.  One such accident closed down west bound lanes of Interstate 90 near Coeur D'Alene for two hours.  Other snow accumulations from this event were noteworthy as well.  In the North Idaho Panhandle including the Coeur D'Alene area; 32 inches fell in Rathdrum, 29 inches three miles east of Spirit Lake, 29 inches in Post falls, 28 inches in Rathdrum, 23 inches in Blanchard, 13 inches in Clark Fork, and 8 inches in Bonners Ferry.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains; 17 inches fell five miles west of St. Maries, with 14 inches two miles south of Deary.  In the Idaho Palouse; 21 inches fell five miles north of Moscow, with much less accumulation south of here.

A deep surface low tracked through North Idaho followed by an arctic front passage.  Numerous vehicle collisions occurred in Moscow on the Idaho Palouse.  Snow accumulations ranged mainly from six to ten inches with lighter amounts in the Coeur D'Alene area and on the palouse.  The Naples area however received heavier amounts with reports ranging from 13 to 16 inches.
On January 29 and 30, a passing winter storm system produced heavy snowfall amounts across Clearwater County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather stations and cooperative observers reported new snowfall accumulations of 10 inches at Shanghai Summit, 8 inches at Pierce, and 6 inches at Headquarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southeast Idaho during the evening and morning of the 12th and 13th and brought with it heavy snow and strong winds.  Heavy snow fell in high elevations, with strong winds causing power outages to 15,000 people in Bingham County on the morning of the 13th.  Although heavy snow did not fall in the Snake River Plain, 30 to 40 minor accidents occurred on icy roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven inches of snow fell at the Crab Creek SNOTEL with 21 inches at the White Elephant SNOTEL.  The town of Island Park received 12 inches according to spotters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Pacific storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to all of southeast Idaho.  One to three feet of snow fell in the Central Mountains with 4 to 8 inches in the Snake River Plain with blizzard conditions.  One to two feet were common in the southern and southeast highlands with up to 32 inches in the Upper Snake Highlands.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed east of Declo.  State Highways 39,32,37,46,21, 81, and 77 were all closed.  US Highways 20 and 26 were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds closed state highway 21 from Grandjean Road to Banner Creek Summit.  33 inches of snow fell at the Lost Wood Divide SNOTEL and 18 inches fell at Stinckney Mill SNOTEL.  29 inches fell at Bear Canyon SNOTEL.  12 inches fell at Mill Creek Summit SNOTEL and 19 inches fell at Smiley Mountain SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 8-9, another winter storm moved east across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall and gusty winds in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Transportation reported between 7 and 9.2 inches of new snowfall accumulation between Powell and Lolo Pass. In addition, several automated weather stations nearby reported wind gusts between 23 mph and 29 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm pushed into Southwest Idaho December 21st and 22nd bringing heavy snow including areas of blowing and drifting snow to most locations including the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported anywhere from six inches of snow at McCall to fourteen inches at Yellow Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm pushed into Southwest Idaho December 21st and 22nd bringing heavy snow including areas of blowing and drifting snow to most locations including the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported nine inches of snow at Idaho City and Atlanta to nearly fourteen inches at Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm pushed into Southwest Idaho December 21st and 22nd bringing heavy snow including areas of blowing and drifting snow to most locations including the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported ten to twelve inches of snow with significant drifting at Fairfield and Blaine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm pushed into Southwest Idaho December 21st and 22nd bringing heavy snow including areas of blowing and drifting snow to most locations including the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported four to six inches of snow with significant blowing and drifting from Gooding to Jerome.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most locations in the Lower Treasure Valley including Weiser and Payette received four to seven inches of snow. Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported around the valley by spotters and local law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold north to northeast winds behind an earlier arctic front passage resulted in wind chill readings near or below minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit from Sandpoint to Coeur D'Alene.  Approximately 1,200 customers lost power in Northern Idaho, which was contributed to the cold temperatures.  There were also about 80 reports of frozen pipes.

Cold north to northeast winds behind an earlier arctic front passage resulted in wind chill readings near or below minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit from Sandpoint to Coeur D'Alene.  Approximately 1,200 customers lost power in Northern Idaho, which was contributed to the cold temperatures.  There were also about 80 reports of frozen pipes.

A strong warm front brought heavy snow to much of north Idaho.  Heavy snow load on roof tops in Coeur D'Alene began to cause problems.  An awning above a business fell onto a sidewalk.  A flat sunroof of a home also had a partial roof collapse.  The additional snowfall also contributed to several power outages resulting from trees falling onto power lines, mostly south of Lake Coeur D'Alene.   The snow also led to several slide-offs on U.S. Highway 95 between Moscow and Lewiston.  Storm total snow accumulations ranged mostly from five to nine inches, except for around a foot of accumulation in the Sandpoint, Coeur D'Alene, and St. Maries areas.

A quick but intense low pressure system brought heavy snow to the North Idaho Panhandle and the Coeur D'Alene area.  Hauser, Spirit Lake, Athol, and Sandpoint were hardest hit where snow accumulations ranged from nine inches to a foot.  Snow from this event contributed to a couple roof collapses in the Post Falls and Coeur d'Alene areas.  Several boats at a marina in Coeur d'Alene were also damaged when a section of a roof collapsed from the weight of the snow.

A winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to most of north Idaho.  A front tracked through late on the 20th into the 21st, followed by a cold low pressure system over the area.  Several collisions and slide-offs occurred in the Coeur D'Alene area.  Storm total snow accumulations of four to eight inches with locally higher amounts were recorded.  The higher amounts include 11 inches in Juilaetta in Latah County, and 10 inches six miles north of Coeur D'Alene and two miles north-northeast of Hayden.

A large winter system moving out of Washington state spread heavy snow over the Intermountain West late December 12th and overnight into the 13th as the cold front lifted through the Cascade Mountains into the West Central and Boise Mountains of Idaho. Mountain areas saw six to ten inches of snowfall while valley areas reported four to six inches. Many accidents were reported by law enforcement agencies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers and trained spotters in the valleys reported around four inches at Idaho City and Centerville. Some of the Snotel sites in the mountains recorded six to eight inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm pushed into Southwest Idaho December 21st and 22nd bringing heavy snow including areas of blowing and drifting snow to most locations including the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most locations in the Treasure Valley including Boise and Meridian received four to five inches of snow. Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported around the valley by spotters and local law enforcement.

Mountain areas saw six to ten inches of snowfall while valley areas reported four to six inches. Many accidents were reported by law enforcement agencies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer and trained spotter reports in the valleys ranged from four inches at Cambridge, Cascade and Donnelly to seven at Yellow Pine and eight to nine inches around the McCall area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm blanketed much of Southwest Idaho with six to fifteen inches of snow over the Christmas holiday period from the 24th to the 25th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to fifteen inches of snow fell in the valleys while up to two feet of snow fell in some of the higher mountain locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven to 12 inches of snow were reported by spotters in Ketchum and Hailey.  Heavy SNOTEL amounts were 15 inches at Dollarhide, 16 inches at Galena, 19 inches at Galena Summit, 20 inches at Lost Wood Divide, and 22 inches at Vienna Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Caribou Highlands with 10 inches at Pine Creek Pass, 20 inches at Sedgewick Peak, 15 inches at Sheep Mountain, 10 inches at Wild Horse Divide, and 12 inches reported by a spotter in Wayan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters recorded 6 inches of snow in Burley, 7 inches in Minidoka and 13 inches in Shoshone with extensive road closures including interstate 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front followed by the passage of an upper level low resulted in heavy snow across north Idaho.  Most snow accumulations ranged from five to nine inches.  Some reported amounts include: 9.5 inches five miles north of Moscow, eight inches near Spirit Lake and Athol, five inches in the Sandpoint and Bonners Ferry areas, five inches in Coeur D'Alene, and four to six inches in the Kellogg and St. Maries areas.  Over 1,000 customers lost power in the Coeur D'Alene area from falling debris onto power lines.

Over a foot of snow was reported in the Upper Snake Highlands including 12 inches at Island Park, 14 inches at White Elephant, 22 inches at Black Bear, and 23 inches at Crab Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell above 7,000 feet with 16 inches reported at the Franklin Basin SNOTEL and 17 inches at the Emigrant Summit SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow along with areas of blowing snow to north Idaho.  Snow accumulations ranged mainly between 6 to 10 inches.  In the Lewiston area, most of the heavy snow fell east of Lewiston with the Lenore area picking up six to 10 inches.  Gusty winds up to 40 MPH resulted in blowing snow in Saint Maries, the palouse, and the Coeur D'Alene area.  A state of emergency was declared by the Idaho State Governor for Bonner, Kootenai, Latah, and Boundary counties.  Numerous road and school closures resulted from the storm.  The University of Idaho in Moscow was closed on the 31st, with the closure extended to February 1st.
Another in a series of storms spread moderate to heavy snow across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho. Strong, gusty winds also accompanied this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers in Idaho City and Centerville reported eight and ten inches of new snow, respectively, and a trained spotter near Placerville reported fifteen inches. SNOTEL sites reported from eight to twelve inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another in a series of winter storms to hit Southwest Idaho left many mountain locations with up to thiry inches of snow including areas of significant blowing and drifting snow on the last day in January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many spotters and cooperative observers reported significant snowfall mainly from the early morning to late evening hours of the thirty first. Reports of ten to twenty inches were common and a spotter near Pine reported thirty inches during a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another in a series of winter storms to hit Southwest Idaho left many mountain locations with up to thiry inches of snow including areas of significant blowing and drifting snow on the last day in January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of ten to twelve inches of new snows from spotters and cooperative observers were common including a report of twenty inches near Glendale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 1 and 2, a winter weather system moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall at Lolo Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at Lolo Pass reported 8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 5 and 6, a winter storm moved across North Central Idaho bringing heavy snow to the mountains and blizzard conditions to the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Dixie reported 10 inches of new snow. The automated weather station at Lolo Pass reported 8 inches of new snow. In addition, wind gusts between 29 mph and 50 mph were reported nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in the city of Rockford and interstate 86 closed the evening of the 31st west of Chubbuck.  Amounts of 3 inches were reported in American Falls and 4 inches in Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Only 3 to 5 inches of snow were reported in the Upper Snake River Plain with 3 inches at Idaho Falls, but winds combined with the snow to cause the closure of route 20 west of Idaho Falls through the INL site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow were reported at the Mill Creek Summit and Bear Canyon SNOTEL sites.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands.  Some snow amounts were 13 inches in Ashton, 18 inches in Island Park, and 9 inches at St. Anthony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with heavy snow and strong winds affected all of southeastern Idaho on the last day of January.  Interstates 84 and 86 were closed from American Falls to Burley with snow amounts in excess of 2 feet falling in the southern highlands above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell in the South Central Highlands with 15 inches reported by spotters in Holbrook.  Over two feet of snow fell at elevations above 7500 feet with 28 inches at the Bostetter SNOTEL and 30 inches at the Howell Canyon SNOTEL.  Fourteen inches fell at Oxford Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a long series of winter storms to hit Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho at the end of January brought various weather conditions to the area including gusty winds with rain and heavy mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers and SNOTEL sites reported six to twelve inches of new snow around the McCall and New Meadows area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front moved just south of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho spreading up to seventeen inches of new snow in the Boise mountains and up to nine inches in the Owyhee mountains. Valley locations received from two to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMVT in Twin Falls reported five inches of new snow. The sheriff's office in Jerome and cooperative observers in Castleford and Buhl reported six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 26 through 28, a very moist Pacific storm system moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall amounts in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Highway 12 corridor between Powell and Lolo Pass received between 7.2 and 9 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 26 through 28, a very moist Pacific storm system moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall amounts in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass received 12 inches of new snow from this event. Significant blowing and drifting snow was reported in Leadore where school was closed for the day due to the winter storm. Wind gusts across the area ranged from 36 mph to 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 5 and 6, a winter storm moved across North Central Idaho bringing heavy snow to the mountains and blizzard conditions to the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Transportation reported blizzard conditions across the Camas Prairie. Highway 95 was closed due to significant blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist low pressure system brought heavy snow to the North Panhandle.  Snow accumulations include five inches near Spirit Lake, and four inches in Hauser and Porthill.
On February 6-8, a persistant winter storm moved east across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall and gusty winds in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The police department in Grangeville reported whiteout conditions across the Camas Prairie from blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front pushed through portions of north central Idaho, bringing accumulating snow, strong winds and blowing and drifting snow reducing visibility. Snow accumulations ranged from 6 inches at the lower elevations, to 9 to 12 inches over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell over the lower elevations, while up to 12 inches fell over the higher terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passage brought a period of brief heavy snow and gusty winds creating blowing snow between 9 PM to midnight on the 14th.  Sustained winds around 25 to 30 mph with gusts to 45 MPH behind the cold front resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow through the early morning hours on the 15th. Most schools in the area were closed or delayed.  Several thousand homes and businesses lost power because of the storm. An estimated three inches of accumulation occurred from this system.

A roof off an old Silver King School building in Smelterville collapsed from the weight of snow.  Two injuries resulted from flying debris during the collapse.  The property damage from this event is not known.

A strong arctic front pushed through portions of north central Idaho, bringing accumulating snow, strong winds and blowing and drifting snow reducing visibility. Snow accumulations ranged from 6 inches at the lower elevations, to 9 to 12 inches over the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to twelve inches of snowfall accumulations reported, including over Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in Lewiston with a wind gust of 59 mph recorded.  These winds downed several trees and power lines.

A winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to most of north Idaho.  A front tracked through late on the 20th into the 21st, followed by a cold low pressure system over the area.  Several collisions and slide-offs occurred in the Coeur D'Alene area.  Storm total snow accumulations of four to eight inches with locally higher amounts were recorded.  The higher amounts include 11 inches in Juilaetta in Latah County, and 10 inches six miles north of Coeur D'Alene and two miles north-northeast of Hayden.

A quick but intense low pressure system brought heavy snow to the North Idaho Panhandle and the Coeur D'Alene area.  Hauser, Spirit Lake, Athol, and Sandpoint were hardest hit where snow accumulations ranged from nine inches to a foot.  Snow from this event contributed to a couple roof collapses in the Post Falls and Coeur d'Alene areas.  Several boats at a marina in Coeur d'Alene were also damaged when a section of a roof collapsed from the weight of the snow.

Yet another moist Pacific storm system interacted with cold air at the surface to produce a significant snowfall event across portions of North Central Idaho with local whiteout conditions reported over the Camas Prairie. Snowfall amounts of 10 to 16 inches were reported with wind gusts up to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to fourteen inches of snow were reported by spotters in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen to fiftenn inches of new snow accumulations was reported by COOP observers within the area.  This included Dixie, ID which measured 15 inches during the period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations between 8 and 12 inches was reported by spotters in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific storm system brought moderate to heavy snow to Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon over a weekend period of February second and third. Gusty winds produced significant blowing and drifting including white out conditions in the mountains and lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of new snow was reported by the cooperative observer in Idaho City and generally ten to twenty inches of new snow fell in Boise and Elmore counties. Twelve inches was reported by a NWS employee at Wilderness Ranch about eleven miles northeast of Boise.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to twelve inches of new snow fell in Adams and Valley counties including eight inches in Cascade as reported by the cooperative observer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Twin Falls, Jerome and Wendell reported four to six inches of new snow with significant blowing and drifting during the overnight and early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff's Office in Grangeville and the Idaho Department of Transportation reported widespread blizzard conditions across the Camas Prairie. Several roads were closed due to significant blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust to 59 mph was recorded at the Grangeville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a cold front over North Central Idaho, a high pressure ridge moved in and resulted in strong night time inversions and very cold overnight temperatures in Lemhi County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures recorded in Salmon, Idaho on January 21, 22 and 23 were -18, -24 and -17 degrees respectively. These cold temperatures resulted in the closure of schools in Salmon and Leadore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 5 and 6, a winter storm moved across North Central Idaho bringing heavy snow to the mountains and blizzard conditions to the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clearwater Tribune reported that the Idaho State Patrol closed US Highway 95 from mile post 223-259 due to a snow avalanche on Whitebird Hill and blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 15 and 16, a vigorous winter storm moved across the Clearwater mountains and produced heavy snow and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass was closed to all but emergency travel during this event. The road crew that plowed the highway to Lolo Pass reported seeing the worst white out conditions in 10 years. The Lodgepole weather observation site reported wind gusts to 38 mph. Red River Hot Springs reported wind gusts to 23 mph. Savage and Lolo Pass received 9 and 8 inches of new snow respectively from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong occluded front brought heavy snow accumulations across portions of North Idaho.  Snow accumulations ranged primarily between five to seven inches, except for Lookout in the Central Idaho Panhandle which received 11 inches of accumulation.

On February 6-8, a persistant winter storm moved east across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall and gusty winds in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho County Free Press reported that the gymnasium roof collapsed under heavy snow loading at the North Idaho Correctional Institution at Cottonwood Butte. 48 inches of snow had accumulated from the recent storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough resided over the Inland Northwest where heavy snow fell across the North Idaho Panhandle.  Schweitzer picked up a foot of snow accumulation, with valley locations from Naples to Porthill picking up four to six inches of accumulation.
On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather stations reported 18 inches at Lolo Pass and 8 inches of new snow at Savage Pass. A cooperative observer at Dixie reported 9 inches of new snow. In addition, gusty winds to 41 mph were reported at the High Camp automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff's Office in Grangeville reported widespread whiteout conditions across the Camas Prairie due to snow and blowing snow. A weather spotter reported substantial blowing and drifting snow. Wind gusts to 59 mph were recorded at the Grangeville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with wind shut down roads across southeastern Idaho.  Heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands with 10 inches at Island Park and Marysville.  US Route 26 was closed from Ririe to the Wyoming state line.  Ten to eighteen inches of snow fell in the central mountains with 18 inches at Dollarhide and 16 inches at Vienna Mine.  Interstate 84 was closed from exit 216 to the Utah state line.  Ten to 12 inches of snow fell at Pine Creek Pass and Sedgewick Peak.  Twelve inches of snow fell at Franklin Basin.  Fourteen inches of snow fell at Mill Creek Summit.  US Highway 20 was closed from Butte City to Idaho Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 20 was closed from Carey to Arco and from Butte City to Idaho Falls.  Zero visibility reported in Taber with 4 foot snow drifts.  Total white out conditions reported in Bingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 3 and 4, a winter weather system moved across the Clearwater Mountains and produced heavy snowfall and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotters in Pierce and Headquarters reported 12 and 6 inches of new snow respectively over a 12 hour period. In addition, nearby automated weather stations reported wind gusts between 21 and 29 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving strong frontal boundary brought heavy snow to all of north Idaho.  Initially temperatures warmed to near or slightly above freezing in the valleys on the 26th through the afternoon on the 27th limiting accumulation potential where a mixture of rain and snow was observed.  Then as the frontal boundary slowly moved east, a round of heavy snow fell in the palouse and the Lewiston area during the late afternoon and evening on the 27th.  Higher elevations received the most significant snowfall due to cooler temperatures and a prolonged period of snow associated with the frontal boundary.  The North and Central Panhandle Mountains received 18 to 29 inches of accumulation.  The valleys of the North Panhandle and the Coeur D'Alene area were also hit hard with 12 to 18 inches of accumulation.  A spotter five miles north of Moscow in the Idaho Palouse received nine inches of snow, with the Winchester area on the Camas Prairie also receiving nine inches.  The Lewiston area received six inches of accumulation.  Numerous road and school closures as well as power outages resulted from the storm.  In Rathdrum, the roof of a business collapsed under the weight of the snow.  In Priest River, the roof of an almost 90 year old building caved in under the weight of more than two feet of snow.  An RV was damaged in the collapse.  In Coeur D'Alene, public schools closed for the first time since 1996.

CZ_NAME: BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Dollarhide and 11 inches fell at Swede Peak in the Big and Little Wood River area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Dollarhide and 11 inches fell at Swede Peak in the Big and Little Wood River area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow and considerable blowing snow to portions of North Idaho.  In the Lewiston area, strong winds led to damage to one building, with areas of blowing snow in the higher elevations surrounding Lewiston.  This blowing snow created brief blizzard like conditions between Lewiston and Moscow which temporarily closed U.S. Highway 95.  The winds also knocked down a power pole in the Lewiston Orchards area.  All schools were closed on the Camas Prairie, with numerous other school closured in North Idaho.  In southern Kootenai county, nearly all roads became impassable after the storm, with many other roads closed in North Idaho.  Peak wind gusts from this event include 59 MPH in Lewiston which occurred at 6:56 AM.  Snow accumulations ranged mainly between three to six inches in valley areas and six to 11 inches in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick snowfall at the rate of 2 to 2.5 inches per hour fell in the Pocatello and Chubbuck areas with amounts as high as 7.5 inches in the benches surrounding the city.  The snow began at 3 pm and amounts of 5 inches were measured by National Weather Service personnel in the cities of Pocatello and Chubbuck by 5:30 pm.  As much as 7.5 inches of snow were measured on the benches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick snowfall at the rate of 2 to 2.5 inches per hour fell in the Pocatello and Chubbuck areas with amounts as high as 7.5 inches in the benches surrounding the city. The snow began at 3 pm and amounts of 5 inches were measured by National Weather Service personnel in the cities of Pocatello and Chubbuck by 5:30 pm. As much as 7.5 inches of snow were measured in the benches by 6 pm. Less than 10 miles west of the city, only 1.5 inches fell at the Pocatello Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving cold front resulted in heavy snow accumulations on the Camas Prairie, where Winchester picked up five inches of snow.
A strong cold front passage resulted in an intense late season snowfall on the Camas Prairie, with Winchester picking up six inches of snow.
A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through North Central Idaho on April 19 through April 20. This storm produced heavy snow accumulations and gusty winds across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Department of Transportation snow plow crew reported 7.0 inches of storm total snow accumulation at Bannock Pass, ID. At Lemhi ridge, located 500 feet above the pass level, 10 inches of snow was reported by an automated snow reporting station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and unstable upslope flow into the Camas Prairie resulted in heavy snow accumulations, with Winchester picking up seven inches.  Snowfall amounts of three to six inches were common above 2500 feet.  In addition to the snow, west to northwest winds gusted to near 45 mph at times resulting in areas of blowing and drifting snow.

An unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system brought heavy snow showers to much of north Idaho.  The unstable atmosphere resulted in widely varying snowfall accumulations across relatively short distances.  In the North Idaho Panhandle, Blanchard picked up ten inches of snow while four inches fell three miles west of Naples. In the Central Panhandle, Lookout Pass picked up 11 inches of accumulation while St. Maries picked up eight inches. In the Coeur D'Alene area, accumulations ranged from four inches to a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures led significant mountain snow melt.  This led to moderate flooding on the Coeur D'Alene and St. Joe Rivers in north Idaho.  The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo crested at 45.96 feet at 9:45 AM PST on May 16th, where the  flood stage is 43.0 feet.  The St. Joe River at Saint Maries crested at 36.94 feet at 2:45 PM PST on May 21st, where the flood stage on the river is 32.5 feet.  In Shoshone county, a boil order was issued due to the river flooding.  Several roads were closed due to the flooding as well.

Strong winds developed after midnight on the 14th with Moburg Canyon getting 9 hours of sustained 40 to 55 mph winds with gusts between 70 and 80 mph.  Strong winds also developed in Pocatello with the benches above the city recording sustained 60 to 65 mph winds around sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moburg Canyon recorded sustained 40 to 55 mph winds for 8 hours with gusts of 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds cause extensive damage throughout Custer and northern Blaine Counties with the brunt of the damage at Redfish Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds cause extensive damage throughout Custer and northern Blaine Counties with the brunt of the damage at Redfish Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds estimated to be as high as 95 mph did extensive damage at the Redfish Lake campgrounds.  Up to 1,000 Lodge-pole pine trees  were uprooted with several snapped 3 to 5 feet above ground level.  Twenty to forty Douglas Firs uprooted as well.  The average diameter of the snapped Lodge-pole pine trees was 5 to 14 inches and the average Douglas Fir diameter was 10 to 24 inches.  At the Glacier View Campground, 3 people camping were injured including one sustaining a broken leg.  All of those injured were taken by ambulance to care facilities.  Campground facilities were damaged with multiple vehicles damaged from the trees falling as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty to 100 lodge-pole pines were uprooted at the Iron Creek Campground.  The average diameter was 4 to 10 inches.  No injuries were reported.

A new washout scar was seen in the wilderness area near Jackson Peak on the Upper North Fork of the Boise River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry cold front passage resulted in damage north of the Bonners Ferry area up to U.S. Highway 95/101 Junction.  Estimated wind gusts to 60 mph resulted in numerous trees falling on houses and mobile homes.  Several strong building roofs were blown off as well.  A building in the Three Mile area that houses county road and bridge equipment was destroyed with damage to 15 vehicles inside.  Bonners Ferry itself recorded a peak wind of 54 MPH.  Several small, short-lived fires resulted from downed power lines.  In Sandpoint, wind damage occurred to an athletic club, along with a downed power line.  Property damage from this event was estimated to be at least $590,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire in Southeast Boise caused by an electrical component near an Idaho Power sub station quickly burned out of control destroying 10 homes and damaging many more including 1 fatality. Another wildfire near Mountain Home burned around 500 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm struck a large tree which fell on a garage and truck in a residential driveway.  The exact timing of this event is not known but occurred in the afternoon.  The property damage is estimated at $2,000.

In Lewiston, downed power lines resulted in several small fires, with minor damage to one outbuilding.  Around 160 customers lost power as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected much of southeast Idaho over the weekend period of October 10th through October 12th.  The heaviest hit areas were the Eastern Magic Valley and southern highlands where snow amounts over a foot were common.  The central mountains also received heavy snow as well as the lower Snake River Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported at high elevation SNOTEL sites.  Twenty inches fell at Mill Creek Summit and 17 inches was recorded at Smiley Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected much of southeast Idaho over the weekend period of October 10th through October 12th.  The heaviest hit areas were the Eastern Magic Valley and southern highlands where snow amounts over a foot were common.  The central mountains also received heavy snow as well as the lower Snake River Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell in the Eastern Magic Valley beginning early in the morning on October 11th and continuing through the morning of the 12th.  Total snow amounts topped out at 17 inches in Rupert, 15 inches in Heyburn, and 13 inches in Burley.  Interstate 84 was closed for an extended period of time from the 11th through the 12th from the Interstate 86 intersection through the Utah border.  Several minor accidents and road slideoffs werer reported by law enforcement throughout the region.

The central mountains also received heavy snow as well as the lower Snake River Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow begain falling early morning on October 11th and continued through the morning of the 12th intermittently.  American Falls recorded 9 inches.  The National Weather Service office at the Pocatello airport recorded 7 inches with 5 to 6 inches in Pocatello and Chubbuck city propers.  Several auto accidents were reported on the icy roads including one fatality near American Falls on interstate 86 near exit 44 on the 11th at 6am.
